description: This course covers the essentials of Spine MRI efficiently via a curated list of cases. The course includes > 20 Spine MRI cases and counting (more coming soon). Check back regularly for updates, including 5+ additional cases coming soon and an introductory video. Happy Learning!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 6 - Synovial Cyst compressing left L4 nerve root

data.text: exiting nerve root, follow this up

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Degenerative disc disease (DDD), most severe at L4/5 and L5/S1. This includes loss of height and dehydration.
  • Facet OA worst at L4/5, with 1.5 cm cyst arising from medial aspect of the left L4-5 facet joint (synovial cyst).
  • Synovial cyst compresses the L4 nerve root at the foramina (also click here to see axial), with severe neuroforaminal stenosis. It also contacts the descending L5 nerve root.
  • Contains hemorrhagic material (see T1 and T2 signal - not simple cystic).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced DDD and facet OA in a previous case. This case shows a good example of facet OA with a larger synovial cyst that causes neuroforaminal stenosis and compresses the exiting nerve root (left L4 in this case), contacting the descending root (L5).


Making the diagnosis: The association (or direct communication) of the cyst with the facet joint cements the diagnosis. T1 and T2 signal are variable -- from pure cystic to hemorrhagic/proteinaceous to calcified. Lastly, when not simple cystic (like in this case), contrast may be required to differentiate from a solid extradural mass.


Treatment may be conservative, percutaneous aspiration, or surgical.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Degenerative disc disease (DDD), most significantly at L1/2 and L2/3
  • Broad-based disc bulges at L1/2 and L2/3 (scroll) with severe spinal canal stenosis (see axial images at L1/2 and L2/3)
  • Compression of the cauda equina nerve roots at these levels (notice cord ends at T12-L1; with cauda equina nerve roots well seen on axial images, surrounded by CSF at that level.
  • As you scroll down to L1/2, notice complete obliteration of all CSF spaces surrounding and compression of the roots
  • Note annular tear depicted by high T2 signal at the disc margin (also see axial)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
"Cauda Equina Syndrome" refers to the clinical syndrome of symptoms that can occur with severe compression of the cauda equina nerve roots. This is important, as the 'syndrome' is not an imaging finding -- it is a clinical diagnosis. Thus, your dictation can describe 'compression of the cauda equina', but not 'cauda equina syndrome'.

Cauda Equina Syndrome:

It is a surgical emergency, and so one of the more common MR spine cases you will read on call. The acute syndrome includes:

  • Saddle Anesthesia (loss of sensation in 'saddle' region -- perineum, buttocks, genitals)
  • Bowel or Bladder Incontinence

What you should know:

  • The most common cause is degenerative (disc disease); but other things can cause it (infection, trauma, inflammation, tumors -- i.e. anything that compresses the cauda equina)

Important Basic Terms:

  • "Spinal Canal Stenosis" refers to narrowing of the spinal canal; when severe can compress the cord/cauda/nerves. Do NOT use "central canal" stenosis. The "central canal" is the tiny CSF space within the cord.
  • "Neuroforaminal Stenosis" refers to narrowing of the neural foramen (where nerves exit laterally). On Sag T1W of lumbar spine, you can see bright T1 signal surrounding the nerve as it exits. Normally, there is fat completely surrounding the exiting nerve roots in the thoracic and lumbar spine. Note that in the cervical spine the exiting nerve roots at the neural foramen are surrounded by venous plexus and not fat.
  • Neuroforaminal stenosis is graded based on loss of this fat signal: Mild (loss of fat signal either vertically or horizontally), Moderate (loss of fat signal surrounding, but without compression), Severe (loss of fat signal surrounding AND compression)

Disc Terminology:

  • "Disc Bulge" refers to more broad-based bulging, > 90 degrees (i.e. greater than 25% of circumference is displaced beyond margins of vertebral bodies). In this case, there is disc bulging (> 90 degrees); also see sag
  • "Disc Herniation" is used when <25% of circumference (i.e. more 'focal'). This is further broken down into:
  • Protrusion - base wider than the dome; remains at same level
  • Extrusion - base narrower than the dome; may extend inferiorly or superiorly -- may migrate or become sequestered (completely separates)

We only introduce these terms here. We will discuss this in greater detail in future cases.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Long segment of cord edema and mild enlargement (longitudinally extensive, > 3 vert bodies). Patchy intramedullary enhancement
  • Prominent flow voids along posterior aspect, seen as prominent veins on the cord surface on MRA (in arterial phase, due to shunting) and on post-gad

Findings are classic for dural AVF.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula (dAVF) is the most common spinal vascular malformation. It's also a classic exam/OSCE question for trainees.

What is it / understanding pathophysiology:

  • Abnormal direct connection between artery and vein in/around the dura. Most often this is spontaneous, but can be due to trauma.
  • Increased venous pressure leads to venous congestion --> dilated venous plexus and impaired drainage that leads to venous congestion / edema in cord. This can lead to cord ischemia.

Imaging Findings reflect this:

  • Cord enlargement and edema (T1 hypo, T2 hyper) diffusely over multiple segments -- the most sensitive finding
  • Prominent serpiginous intradural extramedullary flow voids (dilated venous plexus) -- which is the most specific finding
  • Note that the above findings are a result of the fistula, not the fistula itself.

Angiogram is the gold standard test to confirm diagnosis / can be managed.


Pearl: CSF flow artifact is often seen dorsal to the cord, and can mimic prominent flow voids to the untrained eye. Note that patients with CSF flow artifact and no fistula will not have abnormal signal within the cord. Patients with dAVF will have abnormal cord signal.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 2 - Disc extrusion displacing right S1 nerve root

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Degenerative disc disease (DDD), worst at L5-S1 where there is disk space narrowing and dessictation (dehydration / lower T2 signal)

Subarticular Disc extrusion at L5/S1:

  • Considered a herniation (<90 degrees/25%)
  • Extrusion as more narrow at base compared to dome (NOT 'protrusion')
  • Right central (previously referred to as paracentral) and right subarticular
  • Displacement of descending (traversing) right S1 nerve root, also seen on Sag. This can cause irritation/impingement of the nerve, that leads to symptoms.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are a few important basic concepts to learn here, including disc disease nomenclature (see this link for full details, but we cover the most important below).


Important Definitions:

  • Disc Dessication - dehydration, with loss of normal T2 signal. Very common with aging and DDD.
  • Bulge - disc protrudes beyond margin of vertebral bodies, > 90 degree circumference (like in prior case)
  • Herniation - more focal projection of disc (<90 degrees). Subclassified into:
  • Protrusion (base is wider than the dome)
  • Extrusion (base narrower than the dome). Can migrate away from the disc and detach = "sequestration"


Axial Localization of Disc Herniation:

  • Central
  • Subarticular (level of pedicle, can involve lateral recess)
  • Foraminal (lateral) - at neural foramina
  • Extraforaminal (far lateral) - lateral to neural foramina
  • Anterior

See this excellent diagram. In our case, the extrusion was right central (previously referred to as paracentral) and right subarticular.


The location impacts what nerve can be contacted/impinged:

  • Foraminal -- often involves exiting nerve root at that level
  • Subarticular -- involves the descending (traversing) nerve root in the lateral recess (descending to exit at the NEXT lower level)

See exiting and descending in the foraminal and lateral recess, respectively.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 21 - Cord infarct (Acute Spinal Cord Ischemia)

series.label: Ax t1 tse post gad LOWER
Ax t1 tse post gad UPPER

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Long segment of increased T2 signal in the cord anteriorly (i.e. see here, here)
  • Anterior distribution also seen on axial images, with bilateral dots giving an "owl's eye" appearance (zoom in and scroll to appreciate the anterior distribution and owl's eye).
  • Diffusion restricting

Pattern is classic for acute cord infarct.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Acute spinal cord infarction is rare. Clinical presentation is variable, depending on the part of cord involved. Causes include idiopathic, athero, embolism, trauma, post-op (i.e. post aneurysm stent). Prognosis is poor.


MRI helps make the diagnosis and exclude other causes of symptoms. You should know:

  • Most commonly involves anterior spinal artery distribution -- which results in the classic "owl eye appearance" we see in this case. This involves central cord OR anterior horn high signal bilaterally (given gray matter more susceptible to ischemia = "owl's eye"), sparing posterior cord.
  • Usually long segment
  • Diffusion restricts (though DWI often not acquired unless specifically assessing for ischemia)
  • Can sometimes see vertebral body infarcts adjacent with aortic disease, demonstrating marked T1 hypointensity
  • Like stroke in the brain, it can enhance in the subacute phase

Posterior artery involvement results in unilateral involvement (given the posterior arteries are paired), and is much less common / not classic.

cases.clinicalHistory: Progressive lower extremity neurologic symptoms, r/o infection or demyelination

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Multiple T2 hyperintense lesions in the spinal cord.

  • Mostly cervical (also see here. Also see multiple lesions on axial images: here, here, here, here for example.
  • Short segment (<2 vertebral bodies in height)
  • Less than half the cord cross-section involved, some dorsolateral
  • Demonstrate enhancement (here, here), suggesting acute/active lesions
  • Other subtle chronic lesions are present

This patient had a known history of MS. This is a case of multifocal demyelination. We discuss MS involvement of the cord in more detail below.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Broadly speaking, we can think of cord signal abnormality falling into 5 categories:

  • Tumor (covered earlier)
  • Demyelination
  • Vascular
  • Inflammatory
  • Infectious

This case shows an example of multifocal demyelination, in a pattern that is classic for MS. We introduced MS in the brain MR course.


MS in the spine:

  • Multiple lesions, typically short segment (< 2 vertebral bodies) that commonly impact < half cross-section of cord (often dorsolateral)
  • Cervical cord most common
  • Multiple lesions over space and time
  • Notice not 'expansile'
  • Most often have lesions in the brain as well (see brain MR course)


Classic Differential Considerations (discussed in greater detail in later cases):

  • NMO (Neuromyelitis Optica) - often long segment, involving both sides of the cord, optic nerves involved, brain involvement around ventricles
  • Causes of "Transverse Myelitis" - descriptive term of inflammation of both halves of spinal cord (dorsal and ventral). It is usually secondary to infectious (such as varicella) or inflammatory process (such as vasculitis), but also can be idiopathic TM. Most often long segment, more swollen, more of the cross-section involved.
  • Tumors - usually more expansile, covered previously
  • Cord infarct (discussed separately)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 5 - DDD with Modic Changes, and Facet OA with Synovial Cyst

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Degenerative disc disease (DDD) including multilevel disc dessication, disc space narrowing worst at L5/S1 and L2/3, osteophyte formation. Multifocal disc abnormalities (covered in separate cases)
  • L2/3 Endplate changes are T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense = edema. No fluid signal within the disc space itself.
  • L5/S1 hyperintense on T2W AND T1W sequence (non-FS) = fatty

In addition:

  • Facet OA (multilevel), with joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, osteophyte formation
  • Synovial cyst arising from facet joint (also see sag T2W)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We cover a few concepts related to degenerative spine that you will see again and again, so are important to keep straight. We'll start broad.

"DDD":

  • At the intervertebral disc, which is NOT a synovial joint (so this is NOT 'osteoarthritis').
  • As described, you see: disc desiccation, space narrowing, disc changes previously described, osteophytes
  • Endplate changes (see "Modic" changes below)

Facet Osteoarthritis (OA)

  • At facets, which is a true synovial joint, hence "OA"
  • You see classic findings of OA: cartilage loss, joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, joint effusion, and hypertrophy of the capsule
  • This contributes to foraminal stenosis and lateral recess stenosis (ie see this level), and contribute to spinal canal stenosis as well.

Synovial Cysts arise from facet joints, usually related to facet OA. They can cause symptoms and also contact/compress nerves and contribute to spinal canal stenosis.


Modic Changes:

Endplate changes seen commonly in DDD. It can sound confusing at first with 3 types and different signals, but is quite simple. The numbers (1/2/3) are MUCH less important than understanding the principle:

  • Type 1 - T1 hypo, T2 hyper (EDEMA)
  • Type 2 - hyper T1, hyper T2 (FAT)
  • Type 3 - hypo T1, hypo T2 (SCLEROSIS)

It is much easier to just know that you can get endplate changes that are: edema (active inflammation), fat, or sclerosis. You see these ALL THE TIME.


One Key Principle is differentiating Modic Type 1 (DDD with edema) changes from Osteomyelitis/Discitis (infection). This is covered separately, but with the latter, disc space itself has fluid signal / enhancement; enhancement more avid; more aggressive process.


There is new interest in reporting Modic changes. It is shown that Modic 1 and 2 are associated with veretebrogenic (as opposed to discogenic) cause of low back pain, which can now be treated with ablation of basivertebral nerve.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete replacement of normal fatty marrow in the spine on T1W images at all levels.
  • Pathologic burst fracture of T8 with some retropulsion of posterior wall and narrowing of the thecal sac, without cord compression

In this case, the complete loss of normal fatty marrow signal on T1W images (normally T1 hyperintense) indicates diffuse replacement of the bone marrow.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
T1W images without fat suppression are important to evaluate the bone marrow. The fatty marrow is normally T1 hyperintense -- and so marrow replacing lesions can be identified as hypointense. When the abnormality/replacement is diffuse, it may be more difficult to spot for beginners -- highlighting that this should be specifically evaluated on all MRI spine exams. See below re normal marrow signal and how to differentiate from true lesions.

Normal Marrow Signal:

  • Yellow (fatty) marrow is T1 hyperintense, about 80% fat on average.
  • Red Marrow (hematopoietic) is less fatty and so will be less T1 hyperintense, but still contains some fat (average about 40%). Thus, it will remain hyperintense to disc! Further, it will drop in signal on out-of-phase imaging.
  • In contrast, metastatic disease / marrow replacing lesions will be iso or hypointense to disc, and will not drop in signal on out-phase imaging

In general, kids have a lot of red marrow that converts to yellow in a predictable distribution. But, various conditions can result in "marrow reconversion" of yellow to red marrow in adults (i.e. chronic anemia, diabetes, obesity, cigarettes, living in high altitudes, etc).

When marrow signal is heterogeneous -- you need to compare the low signal areas to disc, and look at in and out-phase imaging to help differentiate from mets.


Diffusely low signal in the bones on T1, less than disc, should make you think of:

  • Diffuse metastases
  • Myeloproliferative and lymphoproliferative disorders
  • Myelofibrosis
  • Treatment with G-CSF (Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor), particularly in cancer patients with anemia
  • Along with other conditions. The history will generally guide the differential.


Vertebral Fractures on MRI:

  • Same as CT regarding height loss, morphology, classification
  • Linear low signal fracture line
  • Look for retropulsion, spinal canal stenosis, and cord compresion/abnormal cord signal associated
  • Acute fractures will have high T2 signal, with FS images most sensitive for that (along with ligamentous injuries).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• 15 mm ovoid lesion at T4/5 level with homogeneous enhancement (compare T1 pre and T1 post contrast)
  • Lesion located intradural (within the thecal sac) but extramedullary (outside of the cord)
  • Wide dural attachment on the left
  • Small amount of signal change in the displaced thoracic cord represenging cord edema (also see here; zoom in)

This is an example of a Meningioma

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The first step in evaluating any spine lesion is determining its location / compartment. This is a fundamental skill that is the precursor to any reasonable differential.

The three compartments are:

  1. Intramedullary (within the cord itself)
  2. Intradural extramedullary (within the thecal sac, but outside of the cord)
  3. Extradural (outside of thecal sac).

Once localized, the differential becomes relatively short (see next section).

teachingSections.name: Differentials
teachingSections.html:
Intradural Extramedullary Lesions:

Lesions within the dura but outside of the cord, often in the subarachnoid space. You may see a CSF cleft between the lesion and the cord.

  • 90% of these are either Nerve Sheath Tumors (schwannoma, neurofibroma) or meningiomas.

Benign Nerve-sheath tumors:

  • Most common intradural extramedullary tumor.
  • May extend extradural with a 'dumbbell' shape along the exiting nerve roots
  • Schwannomas are more common than Neurofibromas (latter mostly associated with NF1), but appear similar on imaging
  • Can enlarge the neural foramina (w lesion extending through foramen)
  • Common cervical and lumbar, including nerve roots.

Meningioma (this case):

  • Mostly benign neoplasm
  • Often a broad dural base like in our case, may have enhancing dural tail
  • May calcify
  • Typically seen on cervical (anterior) / thoracic (posterior); very rare lumbar level.

Others:

  • Myxopapillary ependymoma - a variant of ependymoma that occurs exclusively at the conus medullaris or filum terminale
  • Drop metastases
  • Dermoid (fat containing)
  • Epidermoid (cystic, restricts diffusion)


Extradural Lesions

These arise from outside of the dural sac - including arising from bone/disc. For instance, DDD/disc herniation, epidural spread of tumor or infection are part of this category. We covered these things, including benign bone lesions like aggressive hemangiomas, in an earlier section.

Epidural lipomatosis refers to overgrowth of fat in extradural space that can compress nerves. We see this with steroid excess (exogenous, Cushing's, obesity).


We cover intramedullary lesions separately.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Syndesmophyte formation, referring to ossification of Sharpey fibres (i.e. most peripheral aspect of the anulus fibrosus of the disc). Notice it is vertical, linear, thin, and bridges the vertebral bodies
  • Both SI joints are fused
  • Cervical facet joints are ankylosed (fused)
  • Peripheralization of the cauda equina nerve roots representing arachnoiditis, a rare but devastating complication of AS.

Findings are classic of symmetric seronegative spondyloarthropathy, in this case Ankylosing Spondylitis.

In some cases of AS, scalloping (concavity) of posterior aspect of vertebral body and enlargement of adjacent thecal sac (called dural ectasia) can also be seen.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS) is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy (SpA) -- which refers to a group of MSK conditions that you should know. We introduce the most important axial SpA here and discuss AS in more detail.


Axial SpA tend to present with back pain and morning stiffness. Sacroiliitis and spine changes are the hallmarks on imaging. Typically, break these down into:

A) Symmetric Sacroiliitis - AS, IBD-related spondyloarthropathy

B) Asymmetric Sacroiliitis - Reactive Arthritis, Psoriatic Arthritis.

These will be covered in more detail in MSK courses.


AS is characterized by:

  • Symmetric Sacroiliitis (first see erosions, inflammation, sclerosis centred on SI joints; ankylosis/fusion is end stage). MRI is most sensitive for this.
  • Spondylitis -- initially characterized by inflammation at vertebral body corners (Romanus lesions) which can sclerose (shiny corners); with eventual syndesmophyte formation


Patients are high risk for fracture -- as the entire spine is completely fused. Even minor trauma can result in unstable fractures. CT is most sensitive for these injuries, but CT can be tricky as many of these patients have markedly decreased bone mineralization due to chronic inflammation and medications. Be ready to offer whole spine MRI (with sag T1 and sag T2 FS) if there is suspicion of fracture or even if there is fracture (as there may be more fractures).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Focal distortion of thoracic cord with cord displaced anteriorly and increased CSF space posteriorly
  • "Scalpel sign" with CSF space and focal distortion resembling a scalpel
  • CSF behind the cord in that region is more T2W hyperintense, without CSF pulsation artifact.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Dorsal Thoracic Arachnoid Web is a thickened band of arachnoid over the dorsal cord. This often causes focal cord distortion shown in this case -- with focal indentation of cord and anterior displacement. On imaging, you do not see the web itself.

MRI Findings:

  • "Scalpel Sign" - Thoracic cord focally displaced anteriorly, widening CSF space posteriorly (looks like a surgical scalpel).
  • Also, look at the CSF behind the cord in that region. As there is blockade to normal CSF flow, we don't see CSF pulsation artifact (those subtle clouds of T2 hypointensity) and the CSF here appears more T2W hyperintense.
  • May see T2 signal in cord or syrinx


Classic Ddx to Know:

  • Ventral Cord Herniation - also see focal distortion of cord, but 'pulled' with herniation of cord ventrally at a focal dural defect and often increased T2 signal in the cord. See image below.
  • Dorsal spinal arachnoid cyst - an arachnoid cyst following CSF signal posterior to the cord can look similar, but more 'cyst-like' and no focal distortion of the cord.


Cord Herniation:

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• T4 vertebral body lesion is T1W hypointense and very T2W hyperintense
  • Epidural extension causing severe spinal canal stenosis and cord compression
  • Thickened trabeculae, leading to a corduroy appearance seen on MRI as hypointense linear signal on sag (also see t1), and a polka-dot appearance on axial (also due to thickened trabeculae)
  • Avid enhancement on T1 post-gad, also showing thickened trabeculae
  • Fat containing -- there is no in and out-of-phase imaging here, but sag T2W FS sequence shows low signal, though there is associated artifact

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Vertebral hemangiomas are the most common benign vertebral 'neoplasms'. Like liver hemangiomas, they are not actually neoplasms, and are venous malformations. The vast majority are incidental and of no clinical consequence -- confidently diagnosed on imaging based on appearance on CT (thickened trabeculae / corduroy sign), and MRI (high T1 and T2, often fat containing, enhancing, and thickened trabeculae). Note most of the incidental spine hemangiomas are T1 hyperintense, unlike this case of "aggressive" hemangioma.


Rarely, they can appear "aggressive", like in this case - expanding the vertebral body and with epidural extension. These will often present with symptoms due to nerve / cord compression. Still, confident diagnosis can be made on imaging (same CT and MRI features), demonstrated in this case.


This is a classic mimic of metastatic / malignant disease. Treatment is often surgical decompression +/- post-operative radiation. Pre-operative diagnosis is essential -- including given bleeding risk intra-op.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Expansile mass in the cervical spinal cord at C5/6 level (within the cord, i.e. intramedullary)
  • 2.2 x 1 cm in size, with solid enhancement (compare with pre-contrast T1) and no cystic components
  • Mild associated cord edema
  • No obvious associated hemorrhage

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Step 1 for a spinal mass is to localize the lesion as one of: intramedullary (within cord itself), intradural extramedullary, and extradural. This shortens the differential significantly. In this case, the lesion is clearly intramedullary.


Intramedullary lesions include neoplasms and non-neoplastic abnormalities (i.e. demyelination, etc). Neoplasms typically expand the cord (i.e. 'expansile mass'). Sometimes, non-neoplastic things can also expand the cord (i.e. inflammatory myelitis, cavernoma) but most generally do not.


Intramedullary Neoplasms:

You should know some basic principles, and entities on the ddx.

Principles:

  • Identify levels of cord involved
  • Identify signal / components: solid enhancing components, cystic components, hemorrhage
  • Evaluate margins: circumscribed or infiltrative?

Ddx:

  • Ependymomas and Astrocytomas are the vast majority.
  • Ependymomas predominantly in adults
  • Astrocytomas predominantly in children

While this is a good first thing to learn, we cover these in more detail below.

teachingSections.name: Intramedullary Tumors
teachingSections.html:
Ependymal Tumors (incl. ependymoma)

  • Arise from ependymal lining along central canal (thus "central" in location). Includes ependymoma and its variants, subependymoma.
  • "Ependymoma" is the most common intramedullary cord tumor in adults
  • Enhancing mass with well-defined margins (NOT infiltrative)
  • Expands outward from ependymal lining of central canal
  • Can have cystic or hemorrhagic components, surrounding edema
  • Patients with NF2 may present with multiple cord ependymomas (NF2 introduced in brain MRI course)
  • Treatment is resection when possible

Astrocytic Tumors (incl Astrocytoma)

  • Arise from cord substance itself, so often eccentric to central canal
  • A spectrum of tumors from circumscribed (often lower grade) and infiltrative tumors (often higher grade, with variable enhancement; though more enhancement correlates with higher grade).
  • Astrocytomas are most common intramedullary tumor in childhood.

Hemangioblastoma:

  • Relatively benign (WHO grade 1)
  • Hypervascular enhancing tumor at the pial surface of cerebellum or spinal cord
  • Fluid secretion gives a cyst with nodule appearance in > 50% but may only have subpial enhancing nodule only.
  • The subpial location means they are mostly very peripheral in the cord and may mimic extramedullary tumors.
  • Can present acutely with hemorrhage.


Cord neoplasms are covered in great detail in this excellent radiographics article: https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/rg.2020190196

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 22 - Subacute Combined Degeneration, B12 deficiency

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Increased T2W signal in the dorsal columns of the cord, long segment; giving rise to an 'inverted V' appearance
  • Zoom in to appreciate this and scroll through to appreciate the length
  • See this on Sag images as well (and contrast this with a previous case of cord infarct)

This patient had a history of GI surgery with B12 deficiency. Findings are classic for B12 deficiency causing subacute combined degeneration.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
B12 deficiency can lead to degeneration of the dorsal columns (DCML pathway -- proprioception and vibration sense) -- giving rise to the classic distribution also seen on imaging.

Remembering that is arguably more important than the name "Subacute Combined Degeneration".

Though MRI spine may be normal in many patients with clinical symptoms and B12 deficiency, the classic pattern is:

  • Dorsal Column involvement (inverted V)
  • Can involve lateral corticospinal tract as well (not seen in this case)
  • Typically starts upper thoracic and descends


Though this pattern is classically described with B12, there is a differential including less common entities:

  • Intrathecal methotrexate-induced myelopathy -- this is classic for more 'advanced rounds'
  • Copper deficiency
  • Zinc toxicity
  • HIV myelopathy - may mimic, rare and a late finding in AIDS patients
  • Other causes of TM, but would be highly unusual to be isolated to dorsal columns like this

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Long segment (> 3 vert bodies) of increased T2 signal in the cord with swelling
  • Involves majority of cross-section of the cord

This patient had positive anti-AQP4 antibody.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
NMO (Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder) is a demyelinating disease caused by anti-aquaporin-4 channel antibodies. Knowing that helps understand the distribution of involvement in the brain and cord.

The classic presentation is: optic neuritis, long segment cord involvement ('longitudinally extensive' referring to > 3 vertebral body lengths), and positive antibodies.


Cord Involvement, Typical Pattern:

  • **High T2 involving > 3 vertebral body lengths**
  • Swelling in acute phase
  • Involves most of the cross-section of the cord
  • Often enhances

Contrast this with MS, which is typically short segment (<2 vert bodies), multifocal, and involves < 1/2 of the cord cross-section.


Note that based on imaging alone, this can look like a non-enhancing intramedullary tumor. Therefore, in these cases, clinical history, imaging of the brain, and CSF analysis are very important in narrowing down the Ddx.


When cord T2W hyperintense lesion extends 3+ vertebral bodies, it is referred to as longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesion (LESCL). Ddx includes:

  • Autoimmune disorders of CNS (NMO, ADEM)
  • Infection: HTLV
  • Systemic inflammatory disorders: SLE, Sarcoid
  • Paraneoplastic myelitis
  • Vascular lesion: cord infarct, dAVF


Brain and Orbits: NMO

  • Lesions classically mirror aquaporin-4 water channel distribution, which is mostly periventricular (which makes intuitive sense), including periaqueductal gray matter
  • Periventricular signal is smooth and confluent (unlike MS)
  • Optic neuritis (optic nerves T2 hyperintense, swollen; may atrophy later; may extend back to chiasm)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Intradural, extramedullary lesions at L1 (9 mm), and L4 (3 mm), demonstrating enhancement (compare with T1 pre contrast)

These lesions are consistent with schwannomas

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced the differential for intradural extramedullary lesions previously. These are intradural extramedullary nodules at the lumbar level, including involving nerve roots.

Recall that meningiomas are common in thoracic > cervical spine; and lumbar meningiomas are rare.

teachingSections.name: Intradural Extramedullary Tumors
teachingSections.html:
For a more detailed review of these lesions, see this excellent radiographics article: https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/rg.2019180200 ; key points below.


Meningioma

  • Arise from meningothelial cells of arachnoid, most are WHO grade 1 (benign)
  • Strong female predilection
  • 80% in thoracic spine, 15% cervical spine (lumbar rare at 5%; intra-cord very rare)
  • Often lateral to cord, symptoms arise due to compression of cord/nerves
  • Enhancing, may see dural tail (though harder to see in spine vs brain mening)
  • May calcify, but rare (1-5%) -- but can impact surgery so should note this

Schwannoma

  • Benign (WHO grade 1) nerve sheath tumors, more common in peripheral nerves
  • Involve dorsal sensory nerve roots, so often present with related symptoms (radicular pain)
  • Circumscribed intradural mass with enhancement, often lumbar dorsal sensory nerve roots (like in this case)
  • Neuroforaminal extension -- can have dumbbell morphology, extend extradural; expand foramen
  • Multiple seen in NF2 and schwannomatosis
  • No gender predilection

Neurofibroma

  • Well-demarcated nerve sheath tumor
  • Hallmark of NF1 -- often multiple and bilateral; but may be sporadic (single lesion)
  • Spinal neurofibromas are most common in cervical region
  • Most are asymptomatic
  • Round or fusiform tumors that encase rather than displace nerve roots

Others:

  • Myxopapillary Ependymoma - discussed separately
  • Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor
  • Paraganglioma - can occur at the cauda, similar appearance to paraganglioma elsewhere (flow voids, vascular, etc)
  • Leptomeningeal metastases (drop mets)

cases.clinicalHistory: Incidental cervical spine lesion seen on localizers?

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Ovoid circumscribed expansile intramedullary lesion in the cervical cord at C4
  • Predominantly fluid signal (cystic), with small nodular component that demonstrates solid enhancement
  • The solid enhancing component is at peripheral right surface of cord (subpial), also see T2W
  • Surrounding cord edema

The appearance of this intramedullary lesion is most typical of hemangioblastoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows the classic appearance of spinal hemangioblastoma -- the third most common intramedullary neoplasm (after ependymoma, astrocytoma). Hemangioblastomas are low grade tumors (WHO grade 1). In addition to discussing its appearance, we review the differential for intramedullary lesions below.


What you should know:

  • Most (2/3) are sporadic, 1/3 have VHL -- the latter presenting with multiple tumors. Thus, should image the brain and whole spine to look for additional lesions.
  • Thoracic and cervical cord are most common
  • Eccentric vascular nodule (subpial) is classic, and often have a tumor cyst or syrinx associated. Given vascularity, may have prominent flow voids associated.
  • Can rarely bleed / cause subarachnoid hemorrhage


Intramedullary Spinal Neoplasms:

Recall that intramedullary lesions are within the cord. For now, focus on the three most common: ependymoma, astrocytoma (which together make up 95%), and hemangioblastoma. These have overlapping features, but we review their appearance in this course.

  • Glial Neoplasms (90-95%):
  • Ependymoma (60%) - most common in adults, associated with NF2
  • Astrocytoma (33%) - most common lesion in children
  • Others more rare (ganglioglioma, glioblastoma)
  • Non-Glial
  • Hemangioblastoma
  • Other rare include paraganglioma, metastasis, lymphoma.

Demyelinating lesions can mimic tumor, especially if they are acute and enhancing (i.e. MS lesion).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 17 - Tethered cord, fatty filum, split cord malformation

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Conus is low at L3/4 (below L2), stretching the cord
  • T1 hyperintensity (fat) in the filum terminale
  • Split cord (scroll)
  • Absent posterior elements (like spinous processes and part of lamina) in the sacral spine (unclear here if congenital or prior surgery)
  • Note empty thecal sac sign representing arachnoidits, most likely from prior spine surgery
  • Note fatty atrophy of paraspinal muscles, especially in the sacral spine.

This patient had symptoms compatible with Tethered Cord Syndrome, with imaging confirming the diagnosis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are a few associated congenital findings here that are part of the spectrum of neural tube defects (closed spinal dysraphism). You should be aware of the basic terminology covered below and appearance.


"Tethered Cord Syndrome", like other syndromes, refers to the clinical disorder that is caused by tethering of the cord that restricts movement of the cord.

The imaging manifestations associated include a low lying conus (as seen here, below L2), +/- additional findings depending on the etiology (i.e. thickened filum terminale, fatty filum, split cord, etc). Picture the cord being 'tethered' and pulled down.


"Split Cord Malformation" (aka Diastematomyelia) refers to a longitudinal split in the spinal cord. They are more common in the lower cord. The main thing to know is there are two types: type 1 (with two dural sacs separated by midline bone or cartilage), type 2 (single dural sac, like in this case).


"Fatty Filum Terminale" or lipoma of the filum itself is relatively common -- you will see this on its own incidentally not infrequently. But, it is associated with other findings shown in this case that can lead to tethered cord syndrome (including thickened filum, low lying conus, etc). Note thick filum is considered > 2 mm.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Cauda equina mildly distorted / irregular, with clumping of nerve roots (notice variable thickening of nerve roots, and irregular distribution in the sac as you scroll here). Zoom in to appreciate better.

Findings are typical of arachnoiditis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Arachnoiditis refers to inflammation in the subarachnoid space in general, but people use the term to refer to spinal adhesive arachnoiditis that we demonstrate here. This can be due to infection, post-procedural, or as a delayed complication of inflammatory conditions like ankylosing spondylitis. It is not uncommon post spine surgery.


Imaging findings are clearest in cauda equina:

  • Nerve root clumping together (shown in this case)
  • Empty thecal sac due to roots getting stuck to theca

series.label: C spine T1 sag post gad
T spine T1 sag post gad
L spine T1 sag post gad
Cor obl T1 sacrum post gad
C spine T2 sag post gad
T spine T2 sag post gad
L spine T2 sag post gad

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Intradural, extramedullary mass centred at cauda equina/conus, spanning over 4 vertebral levels
  • T2 hyperintense, T1 isointense
  • Avidly enhancing solid components
  • Sausage shaped, with expansion of spinal canal (scroll)
  • Drop metastasis demonstrated as enhancing tissue at the most caudal aspect of the thecal sac

Findings are typical of Myxopapillary Ependymoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Myxopapillary Ependymoma is a slow-growing variant of ependymoma (WHO grade 2). The most important thing to remember is that these arise from cells in the filum terminale -- and thus classically occur around the conus medullaris and cauda equina (most common primary tumor at this site).


Other important things to know:

  • Frequently extensive mucin (hence T2W hyperintensity), sausage-shaped, encapsulated (like in our case)
  • Span multiple vertebral levels and may expand the spinal canal
  • Involvement of the conus is important to note -- as associated adhesions may preclude complete resection. With partial resection, recurrence is more common of course.

Given low grade, long term survival is good (98% 5-year survival).


DDx: What to Know

  • Schwannoma (often involves cauda and more well defined)
  • Cauda Equina Neuroendocrine Tumor (also known as Paraganglioma) - also arise from filum; look like paragangliomas elsewhere with high vascularity, hemosiderin , flow voids. Can be indistinguishable from myxopapillary ependymomas.

Paragangliomas are very rare! The most common ddx for primary lesions in the cauda are schwannoma and myxopapillary ependymoma. Know the typical appearance described in this case (also a classic exam question).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Fluid signal (high T2) within the disc space and edema (also T1 hypointense) with enhancement (see difference in marrow signal from T1-pre to T1-post contrast) in vertebral bodies of L1 and L2. This is at L1/2 (there are 6 lumbar type vertebrae)
  • Destructive changes on the inferior endplate of L1 and superior endplate of L2. Look for loss of thin dark T1 and T2 cortical line.
  • Enhancement surrounding the fluid and at endplates (compare T1 pre vs post-gad)
  • Paraspinal inflammatory change but no psoas abscess (see pre vs post-gad, T2W though non-FS so less conspicuous)

Findings are in keeping with Discitis-Osteomyelitis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Discitis-Osteomyelitis refers to infection that involves the intervertebral disc AND the adjacent vertebra. Risk factors include prior infection, instrumentation, IV drug use, immunocompromised, endocarditis, and septicemia. Classic presentation is back pain +/- fever.


MRI Findings:

  • **Fluid in disc space (high T2, low T1)
  • Marrow edema in adjacent vertebral bodies (high T2, low T1). The high T2 signal is related to marrow edema, better seen on fat suppressed T2W imaging (STIR, T2 DIXON)
  • Destructive bony changes of adjacent discs
  • May have edema in surrounding tissues (paraspinal, psoas muscle +/- abscess). Presence of abscess in the psoas muscle should always raise suspicion for TB.
  • Enhancement surrounding fluid (rim enhancement) and involving endplates +/- paravertebral
  • Differentiate this appearance from endplate edema often seen with DDD (modic type 1 changes), where there is NO high signal within the disc and much less enhancement if at all.

What else you should know:

  • Complications include epidural abscess, neurologic deficits.
  • Pathophysiology: for kids, infection starts in the disc itself; with adults, actually starts at the endplate.
  • Causative organisms:
  • Staph Aureus most common
  • When TB, called "Pott disease", covered separately.
  • Most are single level, but can involve multiple levels. If multi-level, think about TB!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Intradural flow voids C2-5, both intramedullary and extramedullary, involving cord from C2-5
  • Intranidal aneurysm (see post-gad and pre-gad), with intramedullary hemorrhage associated. Draining veins along anterior aspect. Likely fed by vertebral arteries, but not well demonstrated on this MRA
  • Edema involving medulla and spinal cord down to C4. Syrinx in the cord with hematomyelia (T2 and T1)

Findings are in keeping with a spinal cord AVM complicated by hemorrhage. The culprit aneurysm is also seen.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Spinal AVMs are vascular malformations characterized by arteriovenous shunting. Like AVMs we've introduced elsewhere (including brain MR course), they are characterized by prominent feeding artery, nidus, and early draining vein.


Findings on Spine MRI:

  • Dilated peri-medullary and intramedullary flow voids
  • May have increased T2 signal in cord due to associated edema or myelomalacia (softening of the cord)

May see complications, such as:

  • Hemorrhage (can present acutely) - as seen in this case
  • Progressive myelopathy and cord edema from venous congestion


Basic Concept: Spinal AVM vs Dural AVF

These can be confusing at first. We distinguish them clearly below, and show examples of both in this course.

  • Spinal AVM - arteriovenous shunting with a true nidus classically within the cord.
  • Dural AVFs - abnormal direct connection between artery and vein in or around the dura. This results in increased venous pressure --> venous congestion in the cord itself and dilated perimedullary veins. However, there is no nidus here.
